data.title: Shared artifact impact analysis: consumer discovery in brainstorm scrutiny pipeline
data.description:
## Context

When brainstorm creates an epic that modifies shared artifacts (standards, contracts, configs consumed by multiple agents/modules), the scrutiny pipeline doesn't discover all consumers. The gap analysis runs in the same context window as the understanding loop, creating frame-bias — the agent that accepted a narrow problem framing also evaluates whether the frame is complete. This allowed epic 8df3-61d0 ("Behavioral testing standard") to ship with 4 agents covered when 7+ participate in the test quality lifecycle. The missed consumers caused implementation-plan deadlocks on every instruction-file story for multiple sprint sessions.

## Success Criteria

- The scrutiny pipeline's gap analysis includes Part C (shared artifact impact analysis): scans the codebase for all files referencing artifact(s) being created/modified, cross-references against SCs, flags uncovered consumers. Output format: list of (file_path, matching_line, covered_by_SC: true|false) tuples presented to the user
- Part C triggers when any SC or Approach section references creating or modifying a file that is referenced by 2+ other files outside its own directory — detected by a codebase scan at trigger time. Trigger is tech-stack-agnostic (grep for path references, import statements, source/include directives)
- The Scope fidelity reviewer gains a consumer_completeness dimension — a new dimension on the EXISTING Scope reviewer sub-agent (not a new standalone agent), evaluated using raw Part C scan output (file paths + matching context, not brainstorm-agent summaries)
- consumer_completeness has the same pass threshold (>=4) as all other fidelity dimensions — a low score blocks the epic, it is not averaged with other dimensions
- Smoke test: running brainstorm on an epic with the same description as closed epic 8df3-61d0, Part C enumerates 7+ files referencing behavioral-testing-standard.md and the fidelity reviewer flags any not covered by SCs — verified within the sprint session

## Scenario Analysis

- Part C trigger fails for prose-described changes (critical): Author uses domain-specific language not matching trigger keywords — Part C silently skipped
- Indirect references missed by grep scan (high): Agents referencing artifacts through helper functions or dynamic paths not found
- Frame-bias leaks via curated scan summary (high): If brainstorm agent summarizes scan results before passing to fidelity reviewer, bias propagates — mitigated by passing raw scan output
- Truncated scan results in fidelity reviewer (high): Large consumer lists may exceed sub-agent context limits
- consumer_completeness not weighted as blocking (high): Mitigated by same pass threshold as all fidelity dimensions
- Cross-reference misses partial SC coverage (medium): Consumer mentioned in prose but not in numbered SC
- Config keys not discoverable by file scan (medium): Shared config consumed by hooks not found by path-based scan
- Dead references inflate consumer list (medium): Deprecated modules create false positives

## Dependencies

None. Note: 906c-6d9a (Unify testing philosophy) addresses testing philosophy CONTENT; this epic addresses discovery PROCESS. Complementary, not overlapping. dso-8l5h (Extract sub-agents) unaffected — consumer_completeness is a dimension on the existing Scope reviewer, not a new standalone agent.

## Approach

Two-layer defense: (1) Add Part C to the scrutiny pipeline's gap analysis — tech-stack-agnostic codebase scan (grep/ast-grep for references to modified artifacts) that enumerates consumers, cross-references against SCs, flags uncovered ones. Passes RAW results (file paths + matching context) to downstream consumers, not agent-curated summaries. (2) Add consumer_completeness as a blocking dimension on the existing Scope fidelity reviewer, evaluated by the independent sub-agent receiving raw scan results.
